evals.prompt: Revenue 7.3B/year, COGS 5.1B/year. AR 1.2B, inventory 900M, AP 700M. Compute the CCC and tell me what 10 days of DSO improvement is worth.
evals.expected_output: DSO = 60 days, DIO = 64.4 days, DPO = 50.1 days, CCC = ~74.3 days. One day of revenue = 20M, so 10 DSO days = ~200M cash release.
assertions: Computes DIO on COGS not revenue (approximately 64 days)

evals.prompt: A supplier offers 2/15 net 60 terms. Our overdraft costs 19% p.a. Take the discount or pay on day 60?
evals.expected_output: Discount APR = 0.02/0.98 x 365/45 = ~16.6%. Since 16.6% < 19% cost of funds, paying on day 60 is cheaper - skip the discount. Should show the formula and note the decision flips if funding cost drops below ~16.6%.
assertions: Computes the implied APR of approximately 16.6%
Compares against the 19% funding cost and recommends paying on terms
States the breakeven funding cost where the decision flips

evals.prompt: Our DSO looks stable at 55 days but bad debts are rising. How is that possible and what should I look at?
evals.expected_output: Headline DSO can mask deteriorating aging mix: strong new sales dilute the ratio while old buckets roll toward 90+. Build an aging migration matrix, check roll rates (especially 61-90 to 90+), customer concentration of overdues, and whether write-offs are resetting the aging.
assertions: Explains the dilution effect of sales growth on DSO
Connects roll rates to bad debt provisioning
Checks for write-offs or factoring masking the trend

evals.prompt: From the attached balances (evals/files/wc_balances.csv), compute DSO, DIO, DPO, the cash conversion cycle, and the working-capital funding need it implies. Quantify the cash freed by cutting DSO by 10 days.
evals.expected_output: DSO 60.0, DIO 77.8, DPO 53.7; CCC 84.1 days; funding need $13,278,279; 10 fewer DSO days frees ~$1,578,082.
assertions: DIO approx 78 days and DPO approx 54 days
